title: Python + Hardware
subtitle: Code Meets the Physical World!
overview: This session bridges the gap between software and hardware — students learn how Python code can directly control physical components like LEDs, buzzers, motors, and sensors. Using Raspberry Pi and GPIO pins, the physical world becomes programmable!

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — Raspberry Pi GPIO diagram, Python terminal output, LED circuit wiring, sensor readings printed on screen.

gallery.caption: GPIOZero — The easiest Python library for hardware control!

keyTopics: Python vs Arduino C++ — Same hardware goals, different languages and boards
Raspberry Pi vs Arduino — Full Linux computer (Pi) vs simple microcontroller (Arduino)
GPIO Pins — General Purpose Input/Output: the physical interface between code and components
Digital Output — Python sets a pin HIGH (3.3V) or LOW (0V) to turn LEDs, buzzers ON/OFF
Digital Input — Python reads a pin to detect button presses, PIR motion, switch states
GPIOZero Library — Simple beginner-friendly Python library for controlling GPIO hardware
RPi.GPIO Library — Lower-level Python library giving direct control over GPIO pins
Loops & Delays in Hardware — Using while True and time.sleep() to create blink, pulse, and sense patterns

activity.title: Python Motion Alarm — PIR Sensor + LED + Buzzer with Raspberry Pi
parts: Raspberry Pi (any model with GPIO) or Wokwi simulator
Python 3 installed (pre-installed on Raspberry Pi OS)
steps: Wire PIR sensor: VCC → 5V pin, GND → GND pin, OUT → GPIO 17
Wire LED (with 220Ω resistor): anode → GPIO 27, cathode → GND
Wire buzzer: positive → GPIO 22, negative → GND
Open Terminal on Raspberry Pi and create a new file: nano motion_alarm.py
Import libraries: from gpiozero import MotionSensor, LED, Buzzer and import time
Initialise components: pir = MotionSensor(17), led = LED(27), buzzer = Buzzer(22)
Write the main loop: while True — if pir.motion_detected → led.on(), buzzer.on(), print('Motion Detected!') — else → led.off(), buzzer.off()
Run the program: python3 motion_alarm.py — walk in front of the sensor!
Challenge: Add a time.sleep(2) cooldown so the alarm does not trigger continuously, and print a timestamp with each detection using datetime.now()

quiz.q: What is the main difference between a Raspberry Pi and an Arduino?
options: Raspberry Pi is larger and more expensive only
Raspberry Pi is a full Linux computer capable of running Python and an OS; Arduino is a simple microcontroller that runs one program at a time
Arduino can run Python; Raspberry Pi can only run C++
They are identical in capability and use
quiz.explanation: Raspberry Pi = full Linux computer — runs Python, browsers, games, multiple programs at once. Arduino = simple microcontroller — runs one uploaded sketch at a time. Different tools for different jobs!

options: General Power Input Output
Global Processor Interface Operations
General Purpose Input Output
Graphics Processing and I/O
quiz.explanation: GPIO = General Purpose Input Output! These pins on Raspberry Pi can be programmed as either inputs (read sensors, buttons) or outputs (control LEDs, buzzers, motors) — fully controlled by Python code!

quiz.q: In Python GPIO, what does setting a pin to HIGH mean?
options: The pin is disconnected from the circuit
The pin outputs 3.3V — turning ON whatever component is connected
The pin reads data from a sensor
The pin is set to analog mode
quiz.explanation: HIGH = 3.3V output on Raspberry Pi GPIO! When you set a pin HIGH in Python, it sends 3.3V — enough to light an LED, activate a relay, or signal another component. LOW = 0V (off).

quiz.q: Which Python library is best for BEGINNERS to control GPIO on Raspberry Pi?
options: RPi.GPIO
NumPy
GPIOZero
PySerial
quiz.explanation: GPIOZero is the beginner-friendly library! LED('LED').blink() is all you need to blink an LED. It abstracts the low-level details. RPi.GPIO is more powerful but requires more setup code.

quiz.q: What does this Python code do?  led.on()  time.sleep(1)  led.off()?
options: Reads the LED brightness level
Turns the LED on for 1 second then turns it off
Blinks the LED continuously forever
Checks if the LED is connected correctly
quiz.explanation: led.on() turns the LED on → time.sleep(1) waits 1 second → led.off() turns it off. Simple but powerful! Add a while True loop around it to make it blink forever.

quiz.q: Why do we use time.sleep() in hardware Python programs?
options: To save battery power on the Raspberry Pi
To pause execution for a set duration — controlling timing between actions like blink intervals or sensor polling
To prevent Python from crashing
To slow down the CPU for safety
quiz.explanation: time.sleep(seconds) pauses the program — essential in hardware! Without it, a while True loop runs millions of times per second — LEDs would flicker too fast to see and sensors would be spammed!

quiz.q: What is a PIR sensor used for?
options: Measuring temperature and humidity
Detecting infrared light from the sun
Detecting motion by sensing changes in infrared radiation from moving warm bodies
Measuring distance using sound
quiz.explanation: PIR = Passive Infrared sensor! It detects the infrared heat emitted by moving warm objects (humans, animals). When motion is detected, it sends a HIGH signal to the GPIO pin — perfect for alarm systems!

quiz.q: What does while True: mean in a Python hardware program?
options: The loop runs exactly once
The loop runs until the battery dies
The loop runs continuously and forever until the program is manually stopped
The loop only runs while a button is pressed
quiz.explanation: while True: = infinite loop! The code inside runs over and over forever — perfect for hardware that must constantly check sensors or respond to input. Stop it with Ctrl+C in the terminal.

quiz.q: What is the voltage level of a HIGH GPIO output pin on Raspberry Pi?
options: 5V
quiz.explanation: Raspberry Pi GPIO pins output 3.3V when set HIGH — NOT 5V like Arduino! This is important when connecting components. Some sensors need 5V for VCC but only output 3.3V logic — always check datasheets!

quiz.q: What is the purpose of a resistor when connecting an LED to a GPIO pin?
options: To make the LED glow brighter
To filter out noise from the GPIO pin
To limit current flow and protect the LED and GPIO pin from burning out
To convert 3.3V to 5V for the LED
quiz.explanation: Resistor = current limiter! Without it, too much current flows through the LED and it burns out — and can damage the GPIO pin too! A 330Ω resistor is commonly used with LEDs on Raspberry Pi.

quiz.q: How do you read a digital INPUT (like a button) using GPIOZero in Python?
options: led = LED(17) and check led.value
button = Button(17) and check button.is_pressed
sensor = Sensor(17) and check sensor.read()
pin = GPIO.input(17) and check pin.state
quiz.explanation: With GPIOZero: button = Button(17) creates a button object on GPIO 17. Then button.is_pressed returns True when pressed, False when not. Clean and simple Python hardware reading!

quiz.q: What is the difference between Python on Raspberry Pi and C++ on Arduino for hardware control?
options: Python is faster; C++ is slower
They cannot control the same types of components
Python on Pi is easier to write and supports complex logic, networking, and libraries; Arduino C++ is faster at real-time tasks with less overhead
Arduino C++ can only blink LEDs; Python can do everything
quiz.explanation: Python on Pi = flexible, easy, great for complex projects with networking, AI, and data. Arduino C++ = faster, better for real-time precise timing tasks like PWM and interrupt-driven projects. Choose the right tool!

quiz.q: What does the RPi.GPIO library's GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) do?
options: Sets the Raspberry Pi to Bluetooth communication mode
Tells Python to use BCM (Broadcom chip) GPIO numbering — the numbers printed on GPIO pinout diagrams
Activates all GPIO pins at once
Sets all pins to output mode
quiz.explanation: GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) tells RPi.GPIO to use BCM numbering — the numbers on pinout diagrams like GPIO17, GPIO27. The alternative is GPIO.BOARD which uses physical pin positions (1–40).

quiz.q: What happens if you connect a component directly to Raspberry Pi's 5V pin without a resistor?
options: Everything works perfectly
The component glows dimmer than expected
You may exceed the component's voltage rating and damage it or the Pi's GPIO circuitry
The Pi automatically reduces the voltage
quiz.explanation: Raspberry Pi has both 5V and 3.3V power pins, but GPIO logic is 3.3V. Connecting a 3.3V-rated component to 5V can destroy it. Always check component voltage ratings and use resistors for current protection!

quiz.q: Which of these is a real-world project that uses Python + Hardware (Raspberry Pi)?
options: A website built with HTML and CSS
A mobile game developed in Unity
A home security camera system that detects motion, captures photos, and emails alerts automatically
A spreadsheet that calculates student grades
quiz.explanation: Python + Raspberry Pi = powerful real-world projects! A Pi-powered security camera uses a PIR sensor (detect motion), camera module (capture photo), and Python smtplib (send email alert) — all in one small device!
